    50→---
    51→
    52→## Stories by Category
    53→
    54→### Setup & Infrastructure (US-01)
    55→
    56→#### US-01: Project scaffolding and dev environment
    57→
    58→> As a **developer**, I want to have a well-structured monorepo with shared types, build tooling, and test infrastructure so I can develop the extension and editor packages efficiently.
    59→
    60→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    61→- [ ] Monorepo contains `packages/extension`, `packages/editor`, and `packages/shared` directories
    62→- [ ] `packages/shared` exports TypeScript types for attribution spans, storage schemas, and event definitions
    63→- [ ] Vite builds the editor package with React + TypeScript; output is a chrome-extension page
    64→- [ ] Extension package builds a valid Manifest V3 extension with content script, service worker, and offscreen document entries
    65→- [ ] Vitest runs editor tests; Jest runs extension tests; both pass with zero configuration beyond initial setup
    66→- [ ] Pre-commit hook runs lint + unit tests and completes in under 30 seconds
    67→- [ ] ESLint and Prettier are configured and enforced across all packages
    68→
    69→**Feature:** F0 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→### AI Capture (US-02 through US-05)
    74→
    75→#### US-02: AI response capture from Claude.ai
    76→
    77→> As a **writer**, I want the extension to automatically capture AI responses from my Claude.ai conversations so my editor can later identify which text originated from AI.
    78→
    79→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    80→- [ ] When an AI assistant message finishes streaming on claude.ai, the full response text is stored in chrome.storage.local within 2 seconds
    81→- [ ] Each captured response includes a unique message ID, full text content, and timestamp
    82→- [ ] Only assistant messages are captured — user messages MUST NOT appear in the AI knowledge pool
    83→- [ ] Streaming responses are debounced: capture occurs only after the message stops growing
    84→- [ ] DOM selectors for Claude.ai are isolated in a single module that can be updated without changing capture logic
    85→- [ ] Captured data persists across page navigations within claude.ai
    86→
    87→**Feature:** F1 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
    88→
    89→#### US-03: Copy event capture from Claude.ai
    90→
    91→> As a **writer**, I want the extension to record when I copy text from a Claude.ai response so the editor can later match my pastes to their AI source.
    92→
    93→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    94→- [ ] When the user copies text on a claude.ai page, a copy record is created in chrome.storage.local
    95→- [ ] The copy record contains the selected text, the source message ID, and a timestamp
    96→- [ ] The copy record captures only the selected portion, not the full AI response
    97→- [ ] Multiple copy events from the same message create separate records
    98→- [ ] Copy records are available for paste matching by the editor within 1 second of the copy event
    99→
   100→**Feature:** F2 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   101→
   102→#### US-04: Tab focus tracking
   103→
   104→> As a **writer**, I want my tab switches between Claude.ai and the editor to be logged so I can later see how I split my time between writing and consulting AI.
   105→
   106→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   107→- [ ] Tab switches are logged with timestamps when the user moves between Claude.ai, the editor, and other tabs
   108→- [ ] Both chrome.tabs.onActivated (service worker) and document.visibilitychange (content script) events are captured
   109→- [ ] Tab event logs distinguish between editor tab, Claude.ai tab, and other tabs
   110→- [ ] Tab events persist in chrome.storage.local for later consumption by the analytics system
   111→
   112→**Feature:** F3 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   113→
   114→#### US-05: Clipboard fallback for unlinked pastes
   115→
   116→> As a **writer**, I want the extension to provide clipboard access as a fallback so that pastes not matched to a copy record can still be checked against AI responses.
   117→
   118→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   119→- [ ] An offscreen document is created using chrome.offscreen.createDocument()
   120→- [ ] The offscreen document can read clipboard text via navigator.clipboard.readText()
   121→- [ ] Message passing works: service worker requests clipboard content, offscreen document returns it
   122→- [ ] The offscreen document is created only when needed and cleaned up after use
   123→
   124→**Feature:** F4 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→### Writing & Editing (US-06 through US-08)
   129→
   130→#### US-06: Rich text editor for writing
   131→
   132→> As a **writer**, I want a distraction-free rich text editor that feels like writing on paper so I can focus on my ideas rather than the tool.
   133→
   134→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   135→- [ ] TipTap editor renders inside a chrome-extension:// page with a centered 768px max-width column
   136→- [ ] Editor supports bold, italic, and heading formatting via toolbar buttons and keyboard shortcuts
   137→- [ ] Document model is section-based with paragraph-level TipTap nodes as the atomic unit
   138→- [ ] Custom TipTap marks exist for attribution spans (can store color, confidence, source metadata)
   139→- [ ] Editor accepts paste events and exposes clipboard data for attribution processing
   140→- [ ] Editor functions correctly as a standalone page even when no extension APIs are available
   141→
   142→**Feature:** F5 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   143→
   144→#### US-07: Manuscript aesthetic from day one
   145→
   146→> As a **writer**, I want the editor to have a warm, handcrafted manuscript feel from the start so the tool feels intentional and literary rather than clinical.
   147→
   148→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   149→- [ ] Body text uses Special Elite font; metadata and timestamps use Courier Prime; handwritten elements use Caveat
   150→- [ ] Background uses warm parchment tones (#FBF3E3, #FFF8EF) with subtle paper grain overlay at 5% opacity
   151→- [ ] UI controls (buttons, toggles, inputs) use wired-elements for a sketchy, hand-drawn appearance
   152→- [ ] Borders and decorative shapes use rough.js for hand-drawn rendering
   153→- [ ] Paper texture is generated via css-doodle + feTurbulence SVG filters (no image assets)
   154→- [ ] Cards and panels have slight rotations (-0.5deg to 1.5deg) for a handcrafted feel
   155→
   156→**Feature:** F25 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   157→
   158→#### US-08: Document auto-save and persistence
   159→
   160→> As a **writer**, I want my documents to auto-save as I write so I never lose work, and I want to manage multiple documents from a list view.
   161→
   162→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   163→- [ ] Documents are stored in IndexedDB with their full content, attribution spans, and metadata
   164→- [ ] Auto-save triggers after 2 seconds of edit inactivity (debounced)
   165→- [ ] Rapid edits do not trigger multiple saves — only one save occurs after the debounce period
   166→- [ ] A document list view displays all saved documents with titles and last-modified dates
   167→- [ ] Documents can be opened, created, and deleted from the list view
   168→- [ ] Data persists across browser restarts
   169→
   170→**Feature:** F9 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   171→
   172→---
   173→
   174→### Attribution & Transparency (US-09 through US-16)
   175→
   176→#### US-09: Extension-editor data synchronization
   177→
   178→> As a **writer**, I want my AI conversation data to automatically sync from the extension to the editor so attribution works without manual steps.
   179→
   180→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   181→- [ ] AI knowledge pool entries sync from chrome.storage.local to editor's IndexedDB
   182→- [ ] Copy records sync from chrome.storage.local to editor's IndexedDB
   183→- [ ] Sync occurs via chrome.storage.onChanged for near-real-time updates (extension-page has direct access)
   184→- [ ] A visible connection status indicator shows whether the extension data source is connected
   185→- [ ] When extension APIs are unavailable, the editor continues to function — all new text is treated as GREEN
   186→- [ ] Previously synced data remains available in IndexedDB even if the extension is disconnected
   187→
   188→**Feature:** F10 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   189→
   190→#### US-10: Direct paste detection (RED)
   191→
   192→> As a **writer**, I want text I paste from Claude.ai to be automatically tagged as AI-sourced (RED) so I can see exactly which parts of my document came directly from AI.
   193→
   194→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   195→- [ ] When text is pasted into the editor, it is cross-referenced against copy records in IndexedDB
   196→- [ ] If a matching copy record is found, the pasted span is tagged RED with a link to the source AI message
   197→- [ ] The original paste content is stored in the span metadata for later edit-distance comparison
   198→- [ ] The paste timestamp is recorded as the span's created_at
   199→- [ ] Non-AI pastes (no matching copy record or AI pool entry) are tagged GREEN
   200→
   201→**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   202→
   203→#### US-11: Unlinked paste fallback detection
   204→
   205→> As a **writer**, I want pastes that don't match a copy record to still be checked against AI responses so AI text pasted from other sources is still detected.
   206→
   207→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   208→- [ ] When no copy record matches a paste, substring matching runs against the AI knowledge pool
   209→- [ ] If >80% of the pasted text appears verbatim in any AI pool entry, the span is tagged RED
   210→- [ ] Unlinked matches are stored with a lower confidence score than direct copy-record matches
   211→- [ ] The offscreen clipboard fallback is used when paste event clipboard data is insufficient
   212→- [ ] The threshold (80%) is applied consistently across all unlinked paste checks
   213→
   214→**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   215→
   216→#### US-12: Edit distance tracking and color transitions
   217→
   218→> As a **writer**, I want RED-tagged text to transition to YELLOW and then GREEN as I edit it so I can see my contribution growing in real time.
   219→
   220→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   221→- [ ] Edits within a RED-tagged span trigger Levenshtein distance recomputation against the original paste
   222→- [ ] At 20% character-level edit distance, the span transitions from RED to YELLOW
   223→- [ ] At 70% character-level edit distance, the span transitions from RED/YELLOW to GREEN
   224→- [ ] Re-scoring is debounced to avoid performance degradation during rapid typing
   225→- [ ] Only the edited span is re-scored, not the entire document
   226→- [ ] The original paste content is preserved in metadata even after transition to GREEN
   227→
   228→**Feature:** F7 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   229→
   230→#### US-13: Attribution overlay toggle
   231→
   232→> As a **writer**, I want to toggle an attribution overlay on and off so I can see my document's AI provenance when I want to reflect, and hide it when I want to focus on writing.
   233→
   234→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   235→- [ ] A "Show Attribution" toggle button is visible in the editor UI
   236→- [ ] Attribution overlay is OFF by default
   237→- [ ] When ON, spans are highlighted: GREEN background for original, YELLOW for AI-influenced, RED for direct paste
   238→- [ ] YELLOW spans show a gradient intensity: darker yellow near 20% edit distance, lighter near 70%
   239→- [ ] A summary bar displays the percentage breakdown (e.g., "72% green | 18% yellow | 10% red")
   240→- [ ] Toggling the overlay does not modify the underlying document content
   241→- [ ] The toggle state does not persist across sessions (always starts OFF)
   242→
   243→**Feature:** F8 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   244→
   245→#### US-14: Temporal gating of attribution
   246→
   247→> As a **writer**, I want text I wrote before the AI said something similar to stay GREEN so I get credit for ideas that were mine first.
   248→
   249→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   250→- [ ] Every user text span has a created_at timestamp recorded when the text is first written
   251→- [ ] AI pool matching only considers entries where ai_timestamp < span.created_at
   252→- [ ] If the user typed text before the AI said something similar, the text remains GREEN regardless of similarity score
   253→- [ ] Once text is marked YELLOW (AI said it first), it MUST NOT transition to GREEN via temporal re-evaluation
   254→- [ ] Temporal gating applies to both edit-distance and LLM judge scoring modes
   255→
   256→**Feature:** F13 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   257→
   258→#### US-15: Idea overlap detection (YELLOW)
   259→
   260→> As a **researcher**, I want the editor to detect when my writing paraphrases or restructures ideas that AI surfaced first so I can be transparent about intellectual influence.
   261→
   262→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   263→- [ ] User text is segmented into sentence-level chunks for comparison
   264→- [ ] Each chunk is compared against temporally-gated AI pool entries using 3-5 word n-gram overlap
   265→- [ ] Keyword and entity overlap (capitalized words, technical terms) is used as a secondary signal
   266→- [ ] Combined overlap scores are mapped to a YELLOW intensity gradient
   267→- [ ] Common phrases are filtered out via stopword-aware n-gram exclusion to reduce false positives
   268→- [ ] Scoring runs automatically when new AI pool entries are synced
   269→
   270→**Feature:** F11 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   271→
   272→#### US-16: LLM judge scoring mode
   273→
   274→> As a **researcher**, I want an optional AI-assisted scoring mode that uses Claude Haiku to catch semantic paraphrasing that simple text matching would miss.
   275→
   276→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   277→- [ ] A scoring mode toggle in settings switches between "Edit Distance" (default) and "LLM Judge"
   278→- [ ] In LLM Judge mode, candidate pairs (user chunk + AI pool entry) are batched and sent to Claude Haiku via direct Anthropic API call
   279→- [ ] Haiku returns a 0-1 similarity score and a green/yellow/red classification for each pair
   280→- [ ] Results are cached keyed on content hashes — identical pairs are not re-sent
   281→- [ ] If the Anthropic API is unavailable, scoring falls back to edit-distance mode with a visible notification
   282→- [ ] API key is stored locally in browser storage (acceptable for single-user local tool)
   283→- [ ] API calls are rate-limited to control costs
   284→
   285→**Feature:** F12 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   286→
   287→---
   288→
   289→### Branching & Versioning (US-17 through US-21)
   290→
   291→#### US-17: Git-based version control for documents
   292→
   293→> As a **writer**, I want every save of my document to create a version snapshot so I can always go back to any previous state.
   294→
   295→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   296→- [ ] A git repository is initialized in IndexedDB via isomorphic-git + lightning-fs on first document creation
   297→- [ ] Each auto-save creates a git commit with the document state stored as structured JSON
   298→- [ ] Commit metadata includes timestamp, word count delta from previous commit, and attribution color snapshot
   299→- [ ] Git log returns a chronological list of all commits for a document
   300→- [ ] Git diff between any two commits produces correct additions and deletions
   301→
   302→**Feature:** F14 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   303→
   304→#### US-18: Branch creation from document tree
   305→
   306→> As a **writer**, I want to create a branch of my entire document so I can explore an alternative direction without losing my current version.
   307→
   308→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   309→- [ ] A plus button in the side drawer creates a new branch from the current HEAD commit
   310→- [ ] Branch names are auto-generated (e.g., "branch-1") with an option to rename
   311→- [ ] The side drawer shows a list of all branches with the active branch highlighted
   312→- [ ] Switching branches updates the editor to show that branch's document state
   313→- [ ] The original branch's content is unaffected when editing on a new branch
   314→
   315→**Feature:** F15 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   316→
   317→#### US-19: Branch creation from text selection
   318→
   319→> As a **writer**, I want to branch a specific paragraph so I can try rewriting just that section while keeping the rest of my document intact.
   320→
   321→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   322→- [ ] When the user selects a paragraph, a "Branch this section" action is available (context menu or floating button)
   323→- [ ] Activating the action creates a new branch containing the full document state at that point
   324→- [ ] The branched section in the original document is marked with L-shaped indicators on both sides
   325→- [ ] Branching is limited to paragraph-level TipTap nodes (no arbitrary text range selection)
   326→- [ ] The user can edit the branched section independently on the new branch
   327→
   328→**Feature:** F15 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   329→
   330→#### US-20: Branch preview for sections
   331→
   332→> As a **writer**, I want to preview how a section reads on different branches so I can compare alternatives before deciding which to keep.
   333→
   334→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   335→- [ ] Branched sections display a horizontal scroller at the bottom
   336→- [ ] Each tab in the scroller corresponds to a branch that modifies that section
   337→- [ ] Clicking a tab shows a read-only preview of the section content on that branch
   338→- [ ] The active branch tab is highlighted with accent color (#4A5E8A)
   339→- [ ] Previewing does not change the current branch or document state
   340→
   341→**Feature:** F16 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   342→
   343→#### US-21: Branch merging
   344→
   345→> As a **writer**, I want to merge a branch back into my main document so I can incorporate the best version of a section.
   346→
   347→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   348→- [ ] Double-clicking a branch number (or using the branch picker) initiates a merge
   349→- [ ] Non-conflicting changes are auto-merged and a merge commit is created
   350→- [ ] Conflicting changes (overlapping edits in the same section) display a conflict UI with both versions
   351→- [ ] The conflict UI provides accept/reject controls per conflicting section
   352→- [ ] After resolving all conflicts, a merge commit is created
   353→- [ ] The merged branch can be deleted after successful merge
   354→
   355→**Feature:** F17 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   356→
   357→---
   358→
   359→### History & Visualization (US-22 through US-24)
   360→
   361→#### US-22: Commit timeline visualization
   362→
   363→> As a **writer**, I want to see a visual timeline of my document's history in a side drawer so I can understand how my writing evolved over time.
   364→
   365→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   366→- [ ] A side drawer ("The Paper Drawer") opens to show a vertical commit timeline
   367→- [ ] Each timeline entry displays timestamp, title, summary, and branch indicator
   368→- [ ] The active commit is highlighted with accent color (#4A5E8A) and glow effect
   369→- [ ] Timeline entries are connected by hand-drawn SVG connector lines (perfect-freehand)
   370→- [ ] Clicking a timeline entry navigates the editor to that commit's document state
   371→- [ ] The timeline updates in real-time as new commits are created
   372→
   373→**Feature:** F18 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   374→
   375→#### US-23: LLM-generated commit summaries
   376→
   377→> As a **writer**, I want each version of my document to have a meaningful title and summary so I can quickly find the version I'm looking for.
   378→
   379→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   380→- [ ] On each commit, the diff is sent to Claude Haiku to generate a short title and 1-sentence summary
   381→- [ ] The generated metadata includes a conceptual description of what changed (not just "added 50 words")
   382→- [ ] Metadata is stored alongside the commit and displayed in the timeline
   383→- [ ] If the Haiku API is unavailable, a fallback title is auto-generated from word count delta + timestamp
   384→- [ ] Previously generated metadata is not re-fetched on subsequent views
   385→
   386→**Feature:** F19 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   387→
   388→#### US-24: Diff scrubbing through history
   389→
   390→> As a **researcher**, I want to scrub through my document's history and see what changed between versions so I can trace the evolution of my arguments.
   391→
   392→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   393→- [ ] A scrub slider or timeline click allows viewing any commit's document state
   394→- [ ] Additions (text in current commit but not previous) are highlighted green
   395→- [ ] Deletions (text in previous commit but not current) are highlighted red
   396→- [ ] Diff highlighting uses the editor's own styling and manuscript aesthetic
   397→- [ ] Navigation between commits is smooth without full page reloads
   398→
   399→**Feature:** F20 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   400→
   401→---
   402→
   403→### Analytics & Insights (US-25 through US-27)
   404→
   405→#### US-25: Behavioral analytics event capture
   406→
   407→> As a **writer**, I want my writing behavior to be silently tracked so the analytics dashboard can show me meaningful patterns about my process.
   408→
   409→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   410→- [ ] An event bus captures: edit events, paste events, deletions, scroll-away-from-cursor, active time, tab switches, AI usage, branch creation, and branch sizes
   411→- [ ] Events are aggregated into per-document per-session summaries
   412→- [ ] Session summaries are stored in IndexedDB
   413→- [ ] Event capture does not noticeably impact editor performance (no visible lag or frame drops)
   414→- [ ] Events include timestamps for timeline reconstruction
   415→
   416→**Feature:** F21 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   417→
   418→#### US-26: Analytics dashboard
   419→
   420→> As a **writer**, I want a dashboard that shows me how I write — my AI dependency, my editing patterns, and my use of branches — so I can reflect on my creative process.
   421→
   422→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   423→- [ ] An attribution summary ratio bar shows green/yellow/red percentages with colored segments
   424→- [ ] A session timeline visualizes writing (blue) vs. AI consultation (orange) vs. idle (gray) periods
   425→- [ ] Editing pattern charts show words written/deleted per session and paste frequency
   426→- [ ] Branch statistics show number of branches created and average branch size
   427→- [ ] Charts use hand-drawn style via chart.xkcd + react-rough-fiber
   428→- [ ] Dashboard is accessible from the bottom nav bar (Insights mode)
   429→
   430→**Feature:** F22 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   431→
   432→#### US-27: Cross-project comparison
   433→
   434→> As a **researcher**, I want to compare my AI dependency across different documents and over time so I can track whether I'm becoming more or less reliant on AI.
   435→
   436→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   437→- [ ] A document list view shows per-document attribution ratios (green/yellow/red percentages)
   438→- [ ] A trend chart shows how green/yellow/red ratios change over time across all documents
   439→- [ ] The visualization supports answering "Am I becoming more or less dependent on AI?"
   440→- [ ] Charts use the hand-drawn chart.xkcd aesthetic consistent with the rest of the UI
   441→
   442→**Feature:** F23 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Could-have
   443→
   444→---
   445→
   446→### Data Management (US-28)
   447→
   448→#### US-28: Settings and configuration
   449→
   450→> As a **writer**, I want a settings panel where I can configure scoring mode, check extension connectivity, manage storage, and export my data.
   451→
   452→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   453→- [ ] Scoring mode toggle switches between Edit Distance (default) and LLM Judge
   454→- [ ] Connected AI platforms section shows Claude.ai connection status (connected/disconnected)
   455→- [ ] An input field accepts the Anthropic API key for LLM judge mode (stored locally)
   456→- [ ] Storage usage is displayed with a cleanup action to free space
   457→- [ ] Data export produces a JSON file containing documents, attribution data, and analytics
   458→- [ ] Settings persist across sessions in IndexedDB
   459→
   460→**Feature:** F24 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   461→
   462→---
   463→
   464→### Navigation & Onboarding (US-29 through US-30)
   465→
   466→#### US-29: Keyboard shortcuts and navigation
   467→
   468→> As a **writer**, I want keyboard shortcuts for common actions and a clear navigation system so I can work efficiently without reaching for the mouse.
   469→
   470→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   471→- [ ] Bottom nav bar provides Write, Branch, and Insights mode switching
   472→- [ ] Bottom nav bar has blur backdrop consistent with manuscript aesthetic
   473→- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts exist for: toggle attribution overlay, create branch, open/close side drawer, save, switch modes
   474→- [ ] Side drawer opens and closes with smooth animation
   475→- [ ] All navigation transitions maintain document scroll position and cursor state
   476→
   477→**Feature:** F26 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   478→
   479→#### US-30: First-time user onboarding
   480→
   481→> As a **writer** new to Manum, I want a guided introduction that explains what the tool does and how attribution works so I can start using it effectively without reading documentation.
   482→
   483→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   484→- [ ] First launch triggers an onboarding flow that explains: what Manum does, how attribution colors work, how branching works
   485→- [ ] Onboarding uses the manuscript aesthetic (hand-drawn elements, warm tones)
   486→- [ ] The onboarding flow can be skipped and accessed again from settings
   487→- [ ] After onboarding, the user lands in the editor with a new empty document
   488→- [ ] The onboarding flow covers extension installation/connection status
   489→
   490→**Feature:** F26 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Could-have

Done. Here's what was delivered:

**Features (`features.md`):** 27 features across 4 phases:
- **Phase 1** (5): Project scaffolding, AI response capture, copy event capture, tab tracking, offscreen clipboard
- **Phase 2** (7): TipTap editor, manuscript aesthetic (upfront per user decision), persistence, data sync, RED attribution, edit distance tracking, overlay UI
- **Phase 3** (7): Temporal gating, YELLOW attribution, LLM judge, git integration, branch creation, preview, merge
- **Phase 4** (8): Commit timeline, LLM commit metadata, diff scrubbing, behavioral analytics, dashboard, cross-project comparison, settings, UI polish & onboarding

**User Stories (`user_stories.md`):** 30 stories across 9 categories with 4-7 testable acceptance criteria each. Three personas: Writer, Researcher, Developer.

**Key decisions incorporated:**
- F25 split: aesthetic foundation in Phase 2 (upfront), polish in Phase 4 (F26)
- F0 added for monorepo scaffolding
- Levenshtein distance specified in F7 acceptance criteria
- Direct Anthropic API in F12/F16
- Extension-page hosting in F5/F6

# Manum Feature Registry

## Overview

27 features organized in 4 phases. Features must be built in dependency order.
Phases map to PRD sprints: Capture Layer, Editor + RED, YELLOW + Branching, Visualization + Analytics + Polish.

### User Decisions Applied

- **Hosting:** Editor runs as a `chrome-extension://` page (simplest chrome.storage access)
- **Codebase:** Monorepo with shared packages (`packages/extension`, `packages/editor`, `packages/shared`)
- **Edit distance:** Character-level Levenshtein distance
- **LLM judge:** Direct Anthropic API calls from browser (no proxy)
- **Aesthetic:** Established upfront from Phase 2 (not deferred to polish phase)

## Dependency Diagram

```
Phase 1: Foundation & Capture
─────────────────────────────
  F0 (Scaffolding)
  ├──► F1 (AI Capture)
  │    └──► F2 (Copy Capture)
  ├──► F3 (Tab Tracking)
  └──► F4 (Offscreen Clipboard)

Phase 2: Editor Core + RED Attribution
──────────────────────────────────────
  F0 ──► F5 (TipTap Editor)
         ├──► F25 (Aesthetic Foundation)
         ├──► F9 (Persistence)
         │    └──► F10 (Ext-Editor Sync) ◄── F1, F2
         │         └──► F6 (RED Attribution)
         │              ├──► F7 (Edit Distance Tracking)
         │              │    └──► F8 (Overlay UI) ◄── F6
         │              └────────┘
         └───────────────────────┘

Phase 3: YELLOW Attribution + Git Branching
───────────────────────────────────────────
  F9 ──► F13 (Temporal Gating)
         └──► F11 (YELLOW Attribution) ◄── F10
              └──► F12 (LLM Judge)

  F9 ──► F14 (Git Integration)
         ├──► F15 (Branch Creation)
         │    ├──► F16 (Branch Preview)
         │    └──► F17 (Branch Merge)
         └────────────────────────┘

Phase 4: Visualization, Analytics & Polish
──────────────────────────────────────────
  F14 ──► F18 (Commit Timeline)
          ├──► F19 (LLM Commit Metadata)
          └──► F20 (Diff Scrubbing)

  F9, F3 ──► F21 (Behavioral Analytics)
             └──► F22 (Analytics Dashboard)
                  └──► F23 (Cross-Project Comparison)

  F12, F10 ──► F24 (Settings Panel)

  F25, F22 ──► F26 (UI Polish & Onboarding)
```

## Feature List

### F0: Project Scaffolding & Build Infrastructure

- **Priority:** 1
- **Phase:** 1 — Foundation & Capture Layer
- **Status:** [ ] Not started
- **Depends on:** None
- **Blocks:** F1, F2, F3, F4, F5
- **User Stories:** US-01
- **Tasks:** TBD
- **PRD Reference:** Architecture Overview (tech stack, monorepo structure, testing tools)
- **Key Deliverables:**
  - Monorepo structure with `packages/extension`, `packages/editor`, `packages/shared`
  - Vite + React + TypeScript configuration for editor package
  - Manifest V3 extension scaffolding (manifest.json, content script entry, service worker, offscreen document)
  - Vitest config (editor), Jest config (extension)
  - ESLint + Prettier configuration
  - Pre-commit hook running tests (< 30 seconds)
  - Shared TypeScript types package (attribution spans, storage schemas, events)

### F1: AI Response Capture

- **Priority:** 2

- **Key Deliverables:**
  - Content script injected on claude.ai pages
  - MutationObserver on conversation container detecting assistant messages
  - Streaming debounce (wait for message to stop growing before capture)
  - AI knowledge pool entries stored in chrome.storage.local with message ID, text, timestamp
  - Isolated selector module for easy updates when Claude.ai DOM changes
  - `unlimitedStorage` permission in manifest

### F2: Copy Event Capture

1→# Manum User Stories
     2→
     3→## Summary
     4→
     5→30 user stories across 8 categories.
     6→
     7→### Personas
     8→
     9→| Persona | Description |
    10→|---------|-------------|
    11→| **Writer** | Reflective writer, essayist, or thinker who uses Claude as part of their creative process. Wants intellectual honesty about which ideas are theirs vs. AI-influenced. |
    12→| **Researcher** | Academic researcher who uses AI to draft papers and explore ideas. Needs clear attribution for academic integrity and wants to track thinking evolution. |
    13→| **Developer** | Developer building or maintaining Manum. Needs reliable infrastructure, testable code, and clear project structure. |
    14→
    15→## Traceability Matrix
    16→
    17→| US ID | Title | Feature | Task(s) | Status |
    18→|-------|-------|---------|---------|--------|
    19→| US-01 | Project scaffolding and dev environment | F0 | TBD | [ ] |
    20→| US-02 | AI response capture from Claude.ai | F1 | TBD | [ ] |
    21→| US-03 | Copy event capture from Claude.ai | F2 | TBD | [ ] |
    22→| US-04 | Tab focus tracking | F3 | TBD | [ ] |
    23→| US-05 | Clipboard fallback for unlinked pastes | F4 | TBD | [ ] |
    24→| US-06 | Rich text editor for writing | F5 | TBD | [ ] |
    25→| US-07 | Manuscript aesthetic from day one | F25 | TBD | [ ] |
    26→| US-08 | Document auto-save and persistence | F9 | TBD | [ ] |
    27→| US-09 | Extension-editor data synchronization | F10 | TBD | [ ] |
    28→| US-10 | Direct paste detection (RED) | F6 | TBD | [ ] |
    29→| US-11 | Unlinked paste fallback detection | F6 | TBD | [ ] |
    30→| US-12 | Edit distance tracking and color transitions | F7 | TBD | [ ] |
    31→| US-13 | Attribution overlay toggle | F8 | TBD | [ ] |
    32→| US-14 | Temporal gating of attribution | F13 | TBD | [ ] |
    33→| US-15 | Idea overlap detection (YELLOW) | F11 | TBD | [ ] |
    34→| US-16 | LLM judge scoring mode | F12 | TBD | [ ] |
    35→| US-17 | Git-based version control for documents | F14 | TBD | [ ] |
    36→| US-18 | Branch creation from document tree | F15 | TBD | [ ] |
    37→| US-19 | Branch creation from text selection | F15 | TBD | [ ] |
    38→| US-20 | Branch preview for sections | F16 | TBD | [ ] |
    39→| US-21 | Branch merging | F17 | TBD | [ ] |
    40→| US-22 | Commit timeline visualization | F18 | TBD | [ ] |
    41→| US-23 | LLM-generated commit summaries | F19 | TBD | [ ] |
    42→| US-24 | Diff scrubbing through history | F20 | TBD | [ ] |
    43→| US-25 | Behavioral analytics event capture | F21 | TBD | [ ] |
    44→| US-26 | Analytics dashboard | F22 | TBD | [ ] |
    45→| US-27 | Cross-project comparison | F23 | TBD | [ ] |
    46→| US-28 | Settings and configuration | F24 | TBD | [ ] |
    47→| US-29 | Keyboard shortcuts and navigation | F26 | TBD | [ ] |
    48→| US-30 | First-time user onboarding | F26 | TBD | [ ] |
    49→
    50→---
    51→
    52→## Stories by Category
    53→
    54→### Setup & Infrastructure (US-01)
    55→
    56→#### US-01: Project scaffolding and dev environment
    57→
    58→> As a **developer**, I want to have a well-structured monorepo with shared types, build tooling, and test infrastructure so I can develop the extension and editor packages efficiently.
    59→
    60→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    61→- [ ] Monorepo contains `packages/extension`, `packages/editor`, and `packages/shared` directories
    62→- [ ] `packages/shared` exports TypeScript types for attribution spans, storage schemas, and event definitions
    63→- [ ] Vite builds the editor package with React + TypeScript; output is a chrome-extension page
    64→- [ ] Extension package builds a valid Manifest V3 extension with content script, service worker, and offscreen document entries
    65→- [ ] Vitest runs editor tests; Jest runs extension tests; both pass with zero configuration beyond initial setup
    66→- [ ] Pre-commit hook runs lint + unit tests and completes in under 30 seconds
    67→- [ ] ESLint and Prettier are configured and enforced across all packages
    68→
    69→**Feature:** F0 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→### AI Capture (US-02 through US-05)
    74→
    75→#### US-02: AI response capture from Claude.ai
    76→
    77→> As a **writer**, I want the extension to automatically capture AI responses from my Claude.ai conversations so my editor can later identify which text originated from AI.
    78→
    79→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    80→- [ ] When an AI assistant message finishes streaming on claude.ai, the full response text is stored in chrome.storage.local within 2 seconds
    81→- [ ] Each captured response includes a unique message ID, full text content, and timestamp
    82→- [ ] Only assistant messages are captured — user messages MUST NOT appear in the AI knowledge pool
    83→- [ ] Streaming responses are debounced: capture occurs only after the message stops growing
    84→- [ ] DOM selectors for Claude.ai are isolated in a single module that can be updated without changing capture logic
    85→- [ ] Captured data persists across page navigations within claude.ai
    86→
    87→**Feature:** F1 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
    88→
    89→#### US-03: Copy event capture from Claude.ai
    90→
    91→> As a **writer**, I want the extension to record when I copy text from a Claude.ai response so the editor can later match my pastes to their AI source.
    92→
    93→**Acceptance Criteria:**
    94→- [ ] When the user copies text on a claude.ai page, a copy record is created in chrome.storage.local
    95→- [ ] The copy record contains the selected text, the source message ID, and a timestamp
    96→- [ ] The copy record captures only the selected portion, not the full AI response
    97→- [ ] Multiple copy events from the same message create separate records
    98→- [ ] Copy records are available for paste matching by the editor within 1 second of the copy event
    99→
   100→**Feature:** F2 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   101→
   102→#### US-04: Tab focus tracking
   103→
   104→> As a **writer**, I want my tab switches between Claude.ai and the editor to be logged so I can later see how I split my time between writing and consulting AI.
   105→
   106→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   107→- [ ] Tab switches are logged with timestamps when the user moves between Claude.ai, the editor, and other tabs
   108→- [ ] Both chrome.tabs.onActivated (service worker) and document.visibilitychange (content script) events are captured
   109→- [ ] Tab event logs distinguish between editor tab, Claude.ai tab, and other tabs
   110→- [ ] Tab events persist in chrome.storage.local for later consumption by the analytics system
   111→
   112→**Feature:** F3 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   113→
   114→#### US-05: Clipboard fallback for unlinked pastes
   115→
   116→> As a **writer**, I want the extension to provide clipboard access as a fallback so that pastes not matched to a copy record can still be checked against AI responses.
   117→
   118→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   119→- [ ] An offscreen document is created using chrome.offscreen.createDocument()
   120→- [ ] The offscreen document can read clipboard text via navigator.clipboard.readText()
   121→- [ ] Message passing works: service worker requests clipboard content, offscreen document returns it
   122→- [ ] The offscreen document is created only when needed and cleaned up after use
   123→
   124→**Feature:** F4 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   125→
   126→---
   127→
   128→### Writing & Editing (US-06 through US-08)
   129→
   130→#### US-06: Rich text editor for writing
   131→
   132→> As a **writer**, I want a distraction-free rich text editor that feels like writing on paper so I can focus on my ideas rather than the tool.
   133→
   134→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   135→- [ ] TipTap editor renders inside a chrome-extension:// page with a centered 768px max-width column
   136→- [ ] Editor supports bold, italic, and heading formatting via toolbar buttons and keyboard shortcuts
   137→- [ ] Document model is section-based with paragraph-level TipTap nodes as the atomic unit
   138→- [ ] Custom TipTap marks exist for attribution spans (can store color, confidence, source metadata)
   139→- [ ] Editor accepts paste events and exposes clipboard data for attribution processing
   140→- [ ] Editor functions correctly as a standalone page even when no extension APIs are available
   141→
   142→**Feature:** F5 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   143→
   144→#### US-07: Manuscript aesthetic from day one
   145→
   146→> As a **writer**, I want the editor to have a warm, handcrafted manuscript feel from the start so the tool feels intentional and literary rather than clinical.
   147→
   148→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   149→- [ ] Body text uses Special Elite font; metadata and timestamps use Courier Prime; handwritten elements use Caveat
   150→- [ ] Background uses warm parchment tones (#FBF3E3, #FFF8EF) with subtle paper grain overlay at 5% opacity
   151→- [ ] UI controls (buttons, toggles, inputs) use wired-elements for a sketchy, hand-drawn appearance
   152→- [ ] Borders and decorative shapes use rough.js for hand-drawn rendering
   153→- [ ] Paper texture is generated via css-doodle + feTurbulence SVG filters (no image assets)
   154→- [ ] Cards and panels have slight rotations (-0.5deg to 1.5deg) for a handcrafted feel
   155→
   156→**Feature:** F25 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   157→
   158→#### US-08: Document auto-save and persistence
   159→
   160→> As a **writer**, I want my documents to auto-save as I write so I never lose work, and I want to manage multiple documents from a list view.
   161→
   162→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   163→- [ ] Documents are stored in IndexedDB with their full content, attribution spans, and metadata
   164→- [ ] Auto-save triggers after 2 seconds of edit inactivity (debounced)
   165→- [ ] Rapid edits do not trigger multiple saves — only one save occurs after the debounce period
   166→- [ ] A document list view displays all saved documents with titles and last-modified dates
   167→- [ ] Documents can be opened, created, and deleted from the list view
   168→- [ ] Data persists across browser restarts
   169→
   170→**Feature:** F9 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   171→
   172→---
   173→
   174→### Attribution & Transparency (US-09 through US-16)
   175→
   176→#### US-09: Extension-editor data synchronization
   177→
   178→> As a **writer**, I want my AI conversation data to automatically sync from the extension to the editor so attribution works without manual steps.
   179→
   180→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   181→- [ ] AI knowledge pool entries sync from chrome.storage.local to editor's IndexedDB
   182→- [ ] Copy records sync from chrome.storage.local to editor's IndexedDB
   183→- [ ] Sync occurs via chrome.storage.onChanged for near-real-time updates (extension-page has direct access)
   184→- [ ] A visible connection status indicator shows whether the extension data source is connected
   185→- [ ] When extension APIs are unavailable, the editor continues to function — all new text is treated as GREEN
   186→- [ ] Previously synced data remains available in IndexedDB even if the extension is disconnected
   187→
   188→**Feature:** F10 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   189→
   190→#### US-10: Direct paste detection (RED)
   191→
   192→> As a **writer**, I want text I paste from Claude.ai to be automatically tagged as AI-sourced (RED) so I can see exactly which parts of my document came directly from AI.
   193→
   194→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   195→- [ ] When text is pasted into the editor, it is cross-referenced against copy records in IndexedDB
   196→- [ ] If a matching copy record is found, the pasted span is tagged RED with a link to the source AI message
   197→- [ ] The original paste content is stored in the span metadata for later edit-distance comparison
   198→- [ ] The paste timestamp is recorded as the span's created_at
   199→- [ ] Non-AI pastes (no matching copy record or AI pool entry) are tagged GREEN
   200→
   201→**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   202→
   203→#### US-11: Unlinked paste fallback detection
   204→
   205→> As a **writer**, I want pastes that don't match a copy record to still be checked against AI responses so AI text pasted from other sources is still detected.
   206→
   207→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   208→- [ ] When no copy record matches a paste, substring matching runs against the AI knowledge pool
   209→- [ ] If >80% of the pasted text appears verbatim in any AI pool entry, the span is tagged RED
   210→- [ ] Unlinked matches are stored with a lower confidence score than direct copy-record matches
   211→- [ ] The offscreen clipboard fallback is used when paste event clipboard data is insufficient
   212→- [ ] The threshold (80%) is applied consistently across all unlinked paste checks
   213→
   214→**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   215→
   216→#### US-12: Edit distance tracking and color transitions
   217→
   218→> As a **writer**, I want RED-tagged text to transition to YELLOW and then GREEN as I edit it so I can see my contribution growing in real time.
   219→
   220→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   221→- [ ] Edits within a RED-tagged span trigger Levenshtein distance recomputation against the original paste
   222→- [ ] At 20% character-level edit distance, the span transitions from RED to YELLOW
   223→- [ ] At 70% character-level edit distance, the span transitions from RED/YELLOW to GREEN
   224→- [ ] Re-scoring is debounced to avoid performance degradation during rapid typing
   225→- [ ] Only the edited span is re-scored, not the entire document
   226→- [ ] The original paste content is preserved in metadata even after transition to GREEN
   227→
   228→**Feature:** F7 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   229→
   230→#### US-13: Attribution overlay toggle
   231→
   232→> As a **writer**, I want to toggle an attribution overlay on and off so I can see my document's AI provenance when I want to reflect, and hide it when I want to focus on writing.
   233→
   234→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   235→- [ ] A "Show Attribution" toggle button is visible in the editor UI
   236→- [ ] Attribution overlay is OFF by default
   237→- [ ] When ON, spans are highlighted: GREEN background for original, YELLOW for AI-influenced, RED for direct paste
   238→- [ ] YELLOW spans show a gradient intensity: darker yellow near 20% edit distance, lighter near 70%
   239→- [ ] A summary bar displays the percentage breakdown (e.g., "72% green | 18% yellow | 10% red")
   240→- [ ] Toggling the overlay does not modify the underlying document content
   241→- [ ] The toggle state does not persist across sessions (always starts OFF)
   242→
   243→**Feature:** F8 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   244→
   245→#### US-14: Temporal gating of attribution
   246→
   247→> As a **writer**, I want text I wrote before the AI said something similar to stay GREEN so I get credit for ideas that were mine first.
   248→
   249→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   250→- [ ] Every user text span has a created_at timestamp recorded when the text is first written
   251→- [ ] AI pool matching only considers entries where ai_timestamp < span.created_at
   252→- [ ] If the user typed text before the AI said something similar, the text remains GREEN regardless of similarity score
   253→- [ ] Once text is marked YELLOW (AI said it first), it MUST NOT transition to GREEN via temporal re-evaluation
   254→- [ ] Temporal gating applies to both edit-distance and LLM judge scoring modes
   255→
   256→**Feature:** F13 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   257→
   258→#### US-15: Idea overlap detection (YELLOW)
   259→
   260→> As a **researcher**, I want the editor to detect when my writing paraphrases or restructures ideas that AI surfaced first so I can be transparent about intellectual influence.
   261→
   262→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   263→- [ ] User text is segmented into sentence-level chunks for comparison
   264→- [ ] Each chunk is compared against temporally-gated AI pool entries using 3-5 word n-gram overlap
   265→- [ ] Keyword and entity overlap (capitalized words, technical terms) is used as a secondary signal
   266→- [ ] Combined overlap scores are mapped to a YELLOW intensity gradient
   267→- [ ] Common phrases are filtered out via stopword-aware n-gram exclusion to reduce false positives
   268→- [ ] Scoring runs automatically when new AI pool entries are synced
   269→
   270→**Feature:** F11 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   271→
   272→#### US-16: LLM judge scoring mode
   273→
   274→> As a **researcher**, I want an optional AI-assisted scoring mode that uses Claude Haiku to catch semantic paraphrasing that simple text matching would miss.
   275→
   276→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   277→- [ ] A scoring mode toggle in settings switches between "Edit Distance" (default) and "LLM Judge"
   278→- [ ] In LLM Judge mode, candidate pairs (user chunk + AI pool entry) are batched and sent to Claude Haiku via direct Anthropic API call
   279→- [ ] Haiku returns a 0-1 similarity score and a green/yellow/red classification for each pair
   280→- [ ] Results are cached keyed on content hashes — identical pairs are not re-sent
   281→- [ ] If the Anthropic API is unavailable, scoring falls back to edit-distance mode with a visible notification
   282→- [ ] API key is stored locally in browser storage (acceptable for single-user local tool)
   283→- [ ] API calls are rate-limited to control costs
   284→
   285→**Feature:** F12 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   286→
   287→---
   288→
   289→### Branching & Versioning (US-17 through US-21)
   290→
   291→#### US-17: Git-based version control for documents
   292→
   293→> As a **writer**, I want every save of my document to create a version snapshot so I can always go back to any previous state.
   294→
   295→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   296→- [ ] A git repository is initialized in IndexedDB via isomorphic-git + lightning-fs on first document creation
   297→- [ ] Each auto-save creates a git commit with the document state stored as structured JSON
   298→- [ ] Commit metadata includes timestamp, word count delta from previous commit, and attribution color snapshot
   299→- [ ] Git log returns a chronological list of all commits for a document
   300→- [ ] Git diff between any two commits produces correct additions and deletions
   301→
   302→**Feature:** F14 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   303→
   304→#### US-18: Branch creation from document tree
   305→
   306→> As a **writer**, I want to create a branch of my entire document so I can explore an alternative direction without losing my current version.
   307→
   308→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   309→- [ ] A plus button in the side drawer creates a new branch from the current HEAD commit
   310→- [ ] Branch names are auto-generated (e.g., "branch-1") with an option to rename
   311→- [ ] The side drawer shows a list of all branches with the active branch highlighted
   312→- [ ] Switching branches updates the editor to show that branch's document state
   313→- [ ] The original branch's content is unaffected when editing on a new branch
   314→
   315→**Feature:** F15 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   316→
   317→#### US-19: Branch creation from text selection
   318→
   319→> As a **writer**, I want to branch a specific paragraph so I can try rewriting just that section while keeping the rest of my document intact.
   320→
   321→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   322→- [ ] When the user selects a paragraph, a "Branch this section" action is available (context menu or floating button)
   323→- [ ] Activating the action creates a new branch containing the full document state at that point
   324→- [ ] The branched section in the original document is marked with L-shaped indicators on both sides
   325→- [ ] Branching is limited to paragraph-level TipTap nodes (no arbitrary text range selection)
   326→- [ ] The user can edit the branched section independently on the new branch
   327→
   328→**Feature:** F15 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   329→
   330→#### US-20: Branch preview for sections
   331→
   332→> As a **writer**, I want to preview how a section reads on different branches so I can compare alternatives before deciding which to keep.
   333→
   334→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   335→- [ ] Branched sections display a horizontal scroller at the bottom
   336→- [ ] Each tab in the scroller corresponds to a branch that modifies that section
   337→- [ ] Clicking a tab shows a read-only preview of the section content on that branch
   338→- [ ] The active branch tab is highlighted with accent color (#4A5E8A)
   339→- [ ] Previewing does not change the current branch or document state
   340→
   341→**Feature:** F16 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   342→
   343→#### US-21: Branch merging
   344→
   345→> As a **writer**, I want to merge a branch back into my main document so I can incorporate the best version of a section.
   346→
   347→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   348→- [ ] Double-clicking a branch number (or using the branch picker) initiates a merge
   349→- [ ] Non-conflicting changes are auto-merged and a merge commit is created
   350→- [ ] Conflicting changes (overlapping edits in the same section) display a conflict UI with both versions
   351→- [ ] The conflict UI provides accept/reject controls per conflicting section
   352→- [ ] After resolving all conflicts, a merge commit is created
   353→- [ ] The merged branch can be deleted after successful merge
   354→
   355→**Feature:** F17 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   356→
   357→---
   358→
   359→### History & Visualization (US-22 through US-24)
   360→
   361→#### US-22: Commit timeline visualization
   362→
   363→> As a **writer**, I want to see a visual timeline of my document's history in a side drawer so I can understand how my writing evolved over time.
   364→
   365→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   366→- [ ] A side drawer ("The Paper Drawer") opens to show a vertical commit timeline
   367→- [ ] Each timeline entry displays timestamp, title, summary, and branch indicator
   368→- [ ] The active commit is highlighted with accent color (#4A5E8A) and glow effect
   369→- [ ] Timeline entries are connected by hand-drawn SVG connector lines (perfect-freehand)
   370→- [ ] Clicking a timeline entry navigates the editor to that commit's document state
   371→- [ ] The timeline updates in real-time as new commits are created
   372→
   373→**Feature:** F18 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have
   374→
   375→#### US-23: LLM-generated commit summaries
   376→
   377→> As a **writer**, I want each version of my document to have a meaningful title and summary so I can quickly find the version I'm looking for.
   378→
   379→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   380→- [ ] On each commit, the diff is sent to Claude Haiku to generate a short title and 1-sentence summary
   381→- [ ] The generated metadata includes a conceptual description of what changed (not just "added 50 words")
   382→- [ ] Metadata is stored alongside the commit and displayed in the timeline
   383→- [ ] If the Haiku API is unavailable, a fallback title is auto-generated from word count delta + timestamp
   384→- [ ] Previously generated metadata is not re-fetched on subsequent views
   385→
   386→**Feature:** F19 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   387→
   388→#### US-24: Diff scrubbing through history
   389→
   390→> As a **researcher**, I want to scrub through my document's history and see what changed between versions so I can trace the evolution of my arguments.
   391→
   392→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   393→- [ ] A scrub slider or timeline click allows viewing any commit's document state
   394→- [ ] Additions (text in current commit but not previous) are highlighted green
   395→- [ ] Deletions (text in previous commit but not current) are highlighted red
   396→- [ ] Diff highlighting uses the editor's own styling and manuscript aesthetic
   397→- [ ] Navigation between commits is smooth without full page reloads
   398→
   399→**Feature:** F20 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   400→
   401→---
   402→
   403→### Analytics & Insights (US-25 through US-27)
   404→
   405→#### US-25: Behavioral analytics event capture
   406→
   407→> As a **writer**, I want my writing behavior to be silently tracked so the analytics dashboard can show me meaningful patterns about my process.
   408→
   409→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   410→- [ ] An event bus captures: edit events, paste events, deletions, scroll-away-from-cursor, active time, tab switches, AI usage, branch creation, and branch sizes
   411→- [ ] Events are aggregated into per-document per-session summaries
   412→- [ ] Session summaries are stored in IndexedDB
   413→- [ ] Event capture does not noticeably impact editor performance (no visible lag or frame drops)
   414→- [ ] Events include timestamps for timeline reconstruction
   415→
   416→**Feature:** F21 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   417→
   418→#### US-26: Analytics dashboard
   419→
   420→> As a **writer**, I want a dashboard that shows me how I write — my AI dependency, my editing patterns, and my use of branches — so I can reflect on my creative process.
   421→
   422→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   423→- [ ] An attribution summary ratio bar shows green/yellow/red percentages with colored segments
   424→- [ ] A session timeline visualizes writing (blue) vs. AI consultation (orange) vs. idle (gray) periods
   425→- [ ] Editing pattern charts show words written/deleted per session and paste frequency
   426→- [ ] Branch statistics show number of branches created and average branch size
   427→- [ ] Charts use hand-drawn style via chart.xkcd + react-rough-fiber
   428→- [ ] Dashboard is accessible from the bottom nav bar (Insights mode)
   429→
   430→**Feature:** F22 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   431→
   432→#### US-27: Cross-project comparison
   433→
   434→> As a **researcher**, I want to compare my AI dependency across different documents and over time so I can track whether I'm becoming more or less reliant on AI.
   435→
   436→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   437→- [ ] A document list view shows per-document attribution ratios (green/yellow/red percentages)
   438→- [ ] A trend chart shows how green/yellow/red ratios change over time across all documents
   439→- [ ] The visualization supports answering "Am I becoming more or less dependent on AI?"
   440→- [ ] Charts use the hand-drawn chart.xkcd aesthetic consistent with the rest of the UI
   441→
   442→**Feature:** F23 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Could-have
   443→
   444→---
   445→
   446→### Data Management (US-28)
   447→
   448→#### US-28: Settings and configuration
   449→
   450→> As a **writer**, I want a settings panel where I can configure scoring mode, check extension connectivity, manage storage, and export my data.
   451→
   452→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   453→- [ ] Scoring mode toggle switches between Edit Distance (default) and LLM Judge
   454→- [ ] Connected AI platforms section shows Claude.ai connection status (connected/disconnected)
   455→- [ ] An input field accepts the Anthropic API key for LLM judge mode (stored locally)
   456→- [ ] Storage usage is displayed with a cleanup action to free space
   457→- [ ] Data export produces a JSON file containing documents, attribution data, and analytics
   458→- [ ] Settings persist across sessions in IndexedDB
   459→
   460→**Feature:** F24 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   461→
   462→---
   463→
   464→### Navigation & Onboarding (US-29 through US-30)
   465→
   466→#### US-29: Keyboard shortcuts and navigation
   467→
   468→> As a **writer**, I want keyboard shortcuts for common actions and a clear navigation system so I can work efficiently without reaching for the mouse.
   469→
   470→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   471→- [ ] Bottom nav bar provides Write, Branch, and Insights mode switching
   472→- [ ] Bottom nav bar has blur backdrop consistent with manuscript aesthetic
   473→- [ ] Keyboard shortcuts exist for: toggle attribution overlay, create branch, open/close side drawer, save, switch modes
   474→- [ ] Side drawer opens and closes with smooth animation
   475→- [ ] All navigation transitions maintain document scroll position and cursor state
   476→
   477→**Feature:** F26 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
   478→
   479→#### US-30: First-time user onboarding
   480→
   481→> As a **writer** new to Manum, I want a guided introduction that explains what the tool does and how attribution works so I can start using it effectively without reading documentation.
   482→
   483→**Acceptance Criteria:**
   484→- [ ] First launch triggers an onboarding flow that explains: what Manum does, how attribution colors work, how branching works
   485→- [ ] Onboarding uses the manuscript aesthetic (hand-drawn elements, warm tones)
   486→- [ ] The onboarding flow can be skipped and accessed again from settings
   487→- [ ] After onboarding, the user lands in the editor with a new empty document
   488→- [ ] The onboarding flow covers extension installation/connection status
   489→
   490→**Feature:** F26 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Could-have
   491→

file.content:
# Manum User Stories

## Summary

30 user stories across 8 categories.

### Personas

| Persona | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Writer** | Reflective writer, essayist, or thinker who uses Claude as part of their creative process. Wants intellectual honesty about which ideas are theirs vs. AI-influenced. |
| **Researcher** | Academic researcher who uses AI to draft papers and explore ideas. Needs clear attribution for academic integrity and wants to track thinking evolution. |
| **Developer** | Developer building or maintaining Manum. Needs reliable infrastructure, testable code, and clear project structure. |

## Traceability Matrix

| US ID | Title | Feature | Task(s) | Status |
|-------|-------|---------|---------|--------|
| US-01 | Project scaffolding and dev environment | F0 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-02 | AI response capture from Claude.ai | F1 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-03 | Copy event capture from Claude.ai | F2 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-04 | Tab focus tracking | F3 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-05 | Clipboard fallback for unlinked pastes | F4 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-06 | Rich text editor for writing | F5 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-07 | Manuscript aesthetic from day one | F25 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-08 | Document auto-save and persistence | F9 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-09 | Extension-editor data synchronization | F10 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-10 | Direct paste detection (RED) | F6 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-11 | Unlinked paste fallback detection | F6 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-12 | Edit distance tracking and color transitions | F7 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-13 | Attribution overlay toggle | F8 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-14 | Temporal gating of attribution | F13 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-15 | Idea overlap detection (YELLOW) | F11 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-16 | LLM judge scoring mode | F12 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-17 | Git-based version control for documents | F14 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-18 | Branch creation from document tree | F15 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-19 | Branch creation from text selection | F15 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-20 | Branch preview for sections | F16 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-21 | Branch merging | F17 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-22 | Commit timeline visualization | F18 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-23 | LLM-generated commit summaries | F19 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-24 | Diff scrubbing through history | F20 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-25 | Behavioral analytics event capture | F21 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-26 | Analytics dashboard | F22 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-27 | Cross-project comparison | F23 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-28 | Settings and configuration | F24 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-29 | Keyboard shortcuts and navigation | F26 | TBD | [ ] |
| US-30 | First-time user onboarding | F26 | TBD | [ ] |

---

## Stories by Category

### Setup & Infrastructure (US-01)

#### US-01: Project scaffolding and dev environment

> As a **developer**, I want to have a well-structured monorepo with shared types, build tooling, and test infrastructure so I can develop the extension and editor packages efficiently.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Monorepo contains `packages/extension`, `packages/editor`, and `packages/shared` directories
- [ ] `packages/shared` exports TypeScript types for attribution spans, storage schemas, and event definitions
- [ ] Vite builds the editor package with React + TypeScript; output is a chrome-extension page
- [ ] Extension package builds a valid Manifest V3 extension with content script, service worker, and offscreen document entries
- [ ] Vitest runs editor tests; Jest runs extension tests; both pass with zero configuration beyond initial setup
- [ ] Pre-commit hook runs lint + unit tests and completes in under 30 seconds
- [ ] ESLint and Prettier are configured and enforced across all packages

**Feature:** F0 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### AI Capture (US-02 through US-05)

#### US-02: AI response capture from Claude.ai

> As a **writer**, I want the extension to automatically capture AI responses from my Claude.ai conversations so my editor can later identify which text originated from AI.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] When an AI assistant message finishes streaming on claude.ai, the full response text is stored in chrome.storage.local within 2 seconds
- [ ] Each captured response includes a unique message ID, full text content, and timestamp
- [ ] Only assistant messages are captured — user messages MUST NOT appear in the AI knowledge pool
- [ ] Streaming responses are debounced: capture occurs only after the message stops growing
- [ ] DOM selectors for Claude.ai are isolated in a single module that can be updated without changing capture logic
- [ ] Captured data persists across page navigations within claude.ai

**Feature:** F1 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-03: Copy event capture from Claude.ai

> As a **writer**, I want the extension to record when I copy text from a Claude.ai response so the editor can later match my pastes to their AI source.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] When the user copies text on a claude.ai page, a copy record is created in chrome.storage.local
- [ ] The copy record contains the selected text, the source message ID, and a timestamp
- [ ] The copy record captures only the selected portion, not the full AI response
- [ ] Multiple copy events from the same message create separate records
- [ ] Copy records are available for paste matching by the editor within 1 second of the copy event

**Feature:** F2 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-04: Tab focus tracking

> As a **writer**, I want my tab switches between Claude.ai and the editor to be logged so I can later see how I split my time between writing and consulting AI.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Tab switches are logged with timestamps when the user moves between Claude.ai, the editor, and other tabs
- [ ] Both chrome.tabs.onActivated (service worker) and document.visibilitychange (content script) events are captured
- [ ] Tab event logs distinguish between editor tab, Claude.ai tab, and other tabs
- [ ] Tab events persist in chrome.storage.local for later consumption by the analytics system

**Feature:** F3 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have

#### US-05: Clipboard fallback for unlinked pastes

> As a **writer**, I want the extension to provide clipboard access as a fallback so that pastes not matched to a copy record can still be checked against AI responses.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] An offscreen document is created using chrome.offscreen.createDocument()
- [ ] The offscreen document can read clipboard text via navigator.clipboard.readText()
- [ ] Message passing works: service worker requests clipboard content, offscreen document returns it
- [ ] The offscreen document is created only when needed and cleaned up after use

**Feature:** F4 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have

---

### Writing & Editing (US-06 through US-08)

#### US-06: Rich text editor for writing

> As a **writer**, I want a distraction-free rich text editor that feels like writing on paper so I can focus on my ideas rather than the tool.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] TipTap editor renders inside a chrome-extension:// page with a centered 768px max-width column
- [ ] Editor supports bold, italic, and heading formatting via toolbar buttons and keyboard shortcuts
- [ ] Document model is section-based with paragraph-level TipTap nodes as the atomic unit
- [ ] Custom TipTap marks exist for attribution spans (can store color, confidence, source metadata)
- [ ] Editor accepts paste events and exposes clipboard data for attribution processing
- [ ] Editor functions correctly as a standalone page even when no extension APIs are available

**Feature:** F5 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-07: Manuscript aesthetic from day one

> As a **writer**, I want the editor to have a warm, handcrafted manuscript feel from the start so the tool feels intentional and literary rather than clinical.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Body text uses Special Elite font; metadata and timestamps use Courier Prime; handwritten elements use Caveat
- [ ] Background uses warm parchment tones (#FBF3E3, #FFF8EF) with subtle paper grain overlay at 5% opacity
- [ ] UI controls (buttons, toggles, inputs) use wired-elements for a sketchy, hand-drawn appearance
- [ ] Borders and decorative shapes use rough.js for hand-drawn rendering
- [ ] Paper texture is generated via css-doodle + feTurbulence SVG filters (no image assets)
- [ ] Cards and panels have slight rotations (-0.5deg to 1.5deg) for a handcrafted feel

**Feature:** F25 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-08: Document auto-save and persistence

> As a **writer**, I want my documents to auto-save as I write so I never lose work, and I want to manage multiple documents from a list view.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Documents are stored in IndexedDB with their full content, attribution spans, and metadata
- [ ] Auto-save triggers after 2 seconds of edit inactivity (debounced)
- [ ] Rapid edits do not trigger multiple saves — only one save occurs after the debounce period
- [ ] A document list view displays all saved documents with titles and last-modified dates
- [ ] Documents can be opened, created, and deleted from the list view
- [ ] Data persists across browser restarts

**Feature:** F9 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

---

### Attribution & Transparency (US-09 through US-16)

#### US-09: Extension-editor data synchronization

> As a **writer**, I want my AI conversation data to automatically sync from the extension to the editor so attribution works without manual steps.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] AI knowledge pool entries sync from chrome.storage.local to editor's IndexedDB
- [ ] Copy records sync from chrome.storage.local to editor's IndexedDB
- [ ] Sync occurs via chrome.storage.onChanged for near-real-time updates (extension-page has direct access)
- [ ] A visible connection status indicator shows whether the extension data source is connected
- [ ] When extension APIs are unavailable, the editor continues to function — all new text is treated as GREEN
- [ ] Previously synced data remains available in IndexedDB even if the extension is disconnected

**Feature:** F10 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-10: Direct paste detection (RED)

> As a **writer**, I want text I paste from Claude.ai to be automatically tagged as AI-sourced (RED) so I can see exactly which parts of my document came directly from AI.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] When text is pasted into the editor, it is cross-referenced against copy records in IndexedDB
- [ ] If a matching copy record is found, the pasted span is tagged RED with a link to the source AI message
- [ ] The original paste content is stored in the span metadata for later edit-distance comparison
- [ ] The paste timestamp is recorded as the span's created_at
- [ ] Non-AI pastes (no matching copy record or AI pool entry) are tagged GREEN

**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-11: Unlinked paste fallback detection

> As a **writer**, I want pastes that don't match a copy record to still be checked against AI responses so AI text pasted from other sources is still detected.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] When no copy record matches a paste, substring matching runs against the AI knowledge pool
- [ ] If >80% of the pasted text appears verbatim in any AI pool entry, the span is tagged RED
- [ ] Unlinked matches are stored with a lower confidence score than direct copy-record matches
- [ ] The offscreen clipboard fallback is used when paste event clipboard data is insufficient
- [ ] The threshold (80%) is applied consistently across all unlinked paste checks

**Feature:** F6 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have

#### US-12: Edit distance tracking and color transitions

> As a **writer**, I want RED-tagged text to transition to YELLOW and then GREEN as I edit it so I can see my contribution growing in real time.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Edits within a RED-tagged span trigger Levenshtein distance recomputation against the original paste
- [ ] At 20% character-level edit distance, the span transitions from RED to YELLOW
- [ ] At 70% character-level edit distance, the span transitions from RED/YELLOW to GREEN
- [ ] Re-scoring is debounced to avoid performance degradation during rapid typing
- [ ] Only the edited span is re-scored, not the entire document
- [ ] The original paste content is preserved in metadata even after transition to GREEN

**Feature:** F7 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-13: Attribution overlay toggle

> As a **writer**, I want to toggle an attribution overlay on and off so I can see my document's AI provenance when I want to reflect, and hide it when I want to focus on writing.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A "Show Attribution" toggle button is visible in the editor UI
- [ ] Attribution overlay is OFF by default
- [ ] When ON, spans are highlighted: GREEN background for original, YELLOW for AI-influenced, RED for direct paste
- [ ] YELLOW spans show a gradient intensity: darker yellow near 20% edit distance, lighter near 70%
- [ ] A summary bar displays the percentage breakdown (e.g., "72% green | 18% yellow | 10% red")
- [ ] Toggling the overlay does not modify the underlying document content
- [ ] The toggle state does not persist across sessions (always starts OFF)

**Feature:** F8 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-14: Temporal gating of attribution

> As a **writer**, I want text I wrote before the AI said something similar to stay GREEN so I get credit for ideas that were mine first.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Every user text span has a created_at timestamp recorded when the text is first written
- [ ] AI pool matching only considers entries where ai_timestamp < span.created_at
- [ ] If the user typed text before the AI said something similar, the text remains GREEN regardless of similarity score
- [ ] Once text is marked YELLOW (AI said it first), it MUST NOT transition to GREEN via temporal re-evaluation
- [ ] Temporal gating applies to both edit-distance and LLM judge scoring modes

**Feature:** F13 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-15: Idea overlap detection (YELLOW)

> As a **researcher**, I want the editor to detect when my writing paraphrases or restructures ideas that AI surfaced first so I can be transparent about intellectual influence.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] User text is segmented into sentence-level chunks for comparison
- [ ] Each chunk is compared against temporally-gated AI pool entries using 3-5 word n-gram overlap
- [ ] Keyword and entity overlap (capitalized words, technical terms) is used as a secondary signal
- [ ] Combined overlap scores are mapped to a YELLOW intensity gradient
- [ ] Common phrases are filtered out via stopword-aware n-gram exclusion to reduce false positives
- [ ] Scoring runs automatically when new AI pool entries are synced

**Feature:** F11 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-16: LLM judge scoring mode

> As a **researcher**, I want an optional AI-assisted scoring mode that uses Claude Haiku to catch semantic paraphrasing that simple text matching would miss.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A scoring mode toggle in settings switches between "Edit Distance" (default) and "LLM Judge"
- [ ] In LLM Judge mode, candidate pairs (user chunk + AI pool entry) are batched and sent to Claude Haiku via direct Anthropic API call
- [ ] Haiku returns a 0-1 similarity score and a green/yellow/red classification for each pair
- [ ] Results are cached keyed on content hashes — identical pairs are not re-sent
- [ ] If the Anthropic API is unavailable, scoring falls back to edit-distance mode with a visible notification
- [ ] API key is stored locally in browser storage (acceptable for single-user local tool)
- [ ] API calls are rate-limited to control costs

**Feature:** F12 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have

---

### Branching & Versioning (US-17 through US-21)

#### US-17: Git-based version control for documents

> As a **writer**, I want every save of my document to create a version snapshot so I can always go back to any previous state.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A git repository is initialized in IndexedDB via isomorphic-git + lightning-fs on first document creation
- [ ] Each auto-save creates a git commit with the document state stored as structured JSON
- [ ] Commit metadata includes timestamp, word count delta from previous commit, and attribution color snapshot
- [ ] Git log returns a chronological list of all commits for a document
- [ ] Git diff between any two commits produces correct additions and deletions

**Feature:** F14 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-18: Branch creation from document tree

> As a **writer**, I want to create a branch of my entire document so I can explore an alternative direction without losing my current version.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] A plus button in the side drawer creates a new branch from the current HEAD commit
- [ ] Branch names are auto-generated (e.g., "branch-1") with an option to rename
- [ ] The side drawer shows a list of all branches with the active branch highlighted
- [ ] Switching branches updates the editor to show that branch's document state
- [ ] The original branch's content is unaffected when editing on a new branch

**Feature:** F15 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-19: Branch creation from text selection

> As a **writer**, I want to branch a specific paragraph so I can try rewriting just that section while keeping the rest of my document intact.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] When the user selects a paragraph, a "Branch this section" action is available (context menu or floating button)
- [ ] Activating the action creates a new branch containing the full document state at that point
- [ ] The branched section in the original document is marked with L-shaped indicators on both sides
- [ ] Branching is limited to paragraph-level TipTap nodes (no arbitrary text range selection)
- [ ] The user can edit the branched section independently on the new branch

**Feature:** F15 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Must-have

#### US-20: Branch preview for sections

> As a **writer**, I want to preview how a section reads on different branches so I can compare alternatives before deciding which to keep.

**Acceptance Criteria:**
- [ ] Branched sections display a horizontal scroller at the bottom
- [ ] Each tab in the scroller corresponds to a branch that modifies that section
- [ ] Clicking a tab shows a read-only preview of the section content on that branch
- [ ] The active branch tab is highlighted with accent color (#4A5E8A)
- [ ] Previewing does not change the current branch or document state

**Feature:** F16 | **Tasks:** TBD | **Priority:** Should-have
